Catalog Description

Special Topics in... Credit 0 to 4. Selected topics in an identified area of computer

science. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Approval of instructor.

Additional Course Details

Course Description

This course introduces the state-of-the-art machine learning methods for main tasks in 3D

computer vision and computer graphics.  The purpose of the course is to bring the

students to the frontier of machine learning research in 3D computer vision and

computer graphics through lectures, paper reading and presentations, group

discussions, assignments, and individual course projects. As background requirement,

the students are required to have basic knowledge in machine learning, and

familiarity with computer vision or graphics is desirable but not required. There are

no exams.

Among the specific topics to be covered are:

Neural implicit surfaces, signed and unsigned distance functions

Surface reconstruction from point clouds

Neural radiance fields and novel view synthesis, differentiable

rendering

Neural surface reconstruction from images and video

Gaussian splatting for reconstructing radiance fields and surface

geometry. Fast dense SLAM.

Reconstruction of dynamic scenes, deformation fields

Neural Models for human characters, faces and hands. Human

pose estimation

Classification, segmentation and compression of 3D shapes

Generative AI: Diffusion models for 3D content generation

Generative AI: Human motion generation and video generation

Gaussian splatting for reconstructing radiance fields and surface

geometry. Fast dense SLAM.

Reconstruction of dynamic scenes, deformation fields

Neural Models for human characters, faces and hands. Human

pose estimation

Classification, segmentation and compression of 3D shapes

Generative AI: Diffusion models for 3D content generation

Generative AI: Human motion generation and video generation

Large foundations models for vision comprehension and graphics

modeling

Course Prerequisites

As background requirement, the students are required to have basic

knowledge in machine learning. Familiarity with computer vision or graphics

is desirable but not required.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Define the major terms and concepts in neural shape representation and

neural rendering

Determine appropriate methods and network architectures for solving 3D

modeling tasks

Generate 3D radiance fields or surfaces from points or images

Apply known algorithms for solving problems in 3D vision and graphics

Familiar with foundation models for vision and graphics tasks

Summarize existing research literature about a topic in relevant fields

Create new machine learning approaches to address problems of current

interest in 3D vision and computer graphics and evaluate their effectiveness.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Optional

There is no required textbook for this course. Course notes, slides, papers or videos

will be provided in Canvas for the topics covered.

Notes:

There is no required textbook for this course. Course notes, slides, papers or videos will be

provided in Canvas for the topics covered.

Grading Policy

There will be no final exam in this course. But the class expects to meet during that

time for project presentations, so please keep it open (the specific schedule for that

day will be decided later in the semester and will depend on enrollment. The

evaluation will be based on attendance, participation in class discussion and course

projects.

Attendance and Participation:  60%

Course Project: 40%

The course project will require a survey of works focused on a specific topic, possibly

with experiments and in-depth analysis and discussions. The details for these will be

provided separately.

The expected grading scale will be A  ³  90% > B  ³  80% > C  ³  70% > D  ³  60% > F.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

The course project will require a survey of works focused on a specific topic, possibly

with experiments and in-depth analysis and discussions. The details for these will be

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Assignments (e.g., required components of the course project) are due at the

beginning of the class on the due date assigned. Late assignments will be accepted

only if a prior request is made, with acceptable justifications.

Course Schedule
